description: Hand-curated Widespread Panic song origins written in Burnthday's own voice — facts extracted from fan newsletters (Moon Times / The Panicle), band interviews, and the web, then paraphrased into original prose with sources cited. This is facts-into-original-prose, NOT republication. It is a supplement to the Facebook-sourced song-origins.json: entries with mode 'new' are songs not yet in that set; mode 'augment' entries add or correct facts on an existing origin without duplicating what is already there. The build merges these by slug.

conventions.mode: 'new' = a song not in song-origins.json; 'augment' = adds/corrects facts on an existing entry
conventions.sources: Every factual claim is traceable to a listed source. Unverifiable claims are omitted (evidence discipline).
conventions.attribution: Moon Times = official WSP newsletter (Internet Archive); The Panicle = fan transcription (widespread-panic.blogspot.com); interviews credited to outlet + date.

origins.title: North
origins.text: Not a Panic original — “North” belongs to Jerry Joseph, the songwriter Panic has leaned on for some of its darker, sharper material (most famously “Climb to Safety”). Joseph has said the song came together on the move: a lot of it was written in Nicaragua, in a stretch when he was bouncing between Southern California and Central America. What it aches for is an idealized version of the Pacific Northwest — the kind of longing, he admits, that burns out about three rainy days after you actually get there. He remembers working some of the lines out with his friend, the actor Woody Harrelson, around the mid-'90s. Widespread first worked it into the rotation on 9/25/99.

sources.label: Jerry Joseph, “Passion and Pain” interview (Part I)

origins.title: Time Zones
origins.text: Born in the same two-day Athens writing session that produced “Second Skin.” Dave Schools set the ground rules — he told Jerry Joseph to leave his usual subjects (sex, drugs, death, religion, politics) at the door — and the two wrote ten songs, of which this was one. They handed it to the band in spring 2005, fresh off the year-long hiatus. JB caught the To Kill a Mockingbird references buried in Joseph's lyric and, characteristically, went and got the book (and the Cliff Notes) so his own verses would tie back to it. Schools figured “Time Zones” would be the big new anthem and “Second Skin” the slow-burn jam; onstage the two promptly swapped roles. Debuted 4/30/05 and landed on Earth to America.

sources.label: Dave Schools & John Bell, “New Frontiers” by Andy Tennille

origins.title: Her Dance Needs No Body
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (music seeded by Jimmy Herring)
origins.text: One of the first songs to grow out of Jimmy Herring joining the band. JB has credited the musical seed of this one — along with “Three Candles” — to ideas Herring brought in already cooking. Where plenty of Panic tunes are built to wander off, as JB puts it, “into la la land,” he says they decided this one earned a straighter shape: verse, chorus, bridge, solo, out. First played 4/1/08.

origins.title: Raise The Roof
origins.text: Proof that Panic knew when to leave a song alone. JB has said “Raise the Roof” came out of Mikey's direction during the Ain't Life Grand demo sessions — the band followed him through it, cleaned up the demo vocals, and basically left it there. Coming back off a month and a half on the road, they listened again and decided it didn't need a jam bolted onto it: maybe another guitar, maybe a third vocal to fill it out, but in JB's words it “had what it had.” One of the prettier things on the record — and one they mostly shelved after 2001 (45 plays, last on 6/22/01).

sources.label: John Bell, R&R interview, Grady's Restaurant, Atlanta

origins.title: Three Candles
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (music seeded by Jimmy Herring)
origins.text: Another one that traces back to Jimmy Herring settling into the band. JB has pointed to this song — alongside “Her Dance Needs No Body” — as growing out of musical ideas Jimmy brought in already cooking. It debuted 4/1/08 and stayed a relatively rare bird, a few dozen outings before it slipped out of rotation.

origins.title: From The Cradle
origins.text: One of the quieter gut-punches in the catalog, and a Todd Nance song. Its imagery reads like the drowned aftermath of Hurricane Katrina — but, as American Songwriter noted, Nance actually wrote it before the storm ever hit, which somehow only makes it land harder. It surfaced on Earth to America and first reached a stage on 4/22/06.

origins.title: Gomero Blanco
origins.text: A ghost from the very beginning. According to the Panicle, “Gomero Blanco” was cut during the May 1987 sessions at John Keane's Athens studio, around the making of the first Space Wrangler — but it never became a live fixture. The closest most fans ever got were similar-sounding jams during the band's early Uptown Lounge nights. One for the completists still digging through the oldest tapes.

origins.title: Driving Song
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (band-credited; biographically a John Bell / Michael Houser co-write)
origins.text: One of the oldest things in the book, and pure Panic DNA. The band's own history traces it back to John Bell and Michael Houser writing together as University of Georgia roommates in the early '80s — the same partnership that gave up “Chilly Water” before there was even a Widespread Panic to play them. It anchored the 1988 debut, Space Wrangler, and years later Bell and Houser were still trading acoustic “Driving Song” duets. (Everyday Companion credits it band-wide, per Panic's shared-writing convention.)

origins.title: Rock
origins.text: A Panic original that traveled under a second name — the working title “Brass Shoes, Walking Blues.” It first turned up on the 1991 self-titled album fans call “Mom's Kitchen.”

origins.title: Tall Boy
origins.text: Logged on the band's own alias sheet as “Naked In The Mud,” Tall Boy made its studio home on Bombs & Butterflies (1997). The words drop you into a Mississippi Delta swelter — cold can sweating in your hand, sun overhead — which is about all the explanation a song this easy-going needs.

sources.label: Everyday Companion — Song Histories & Originals index

origins.title: Better Off
origins.text: The rare Panic song with an outside name on the credit. It's co-written with T Lavitz — the Dixie Dregs keyboardist who filled the WSP keyboard chair in 1991 and played on their Capricorn debut before the Dregs called him back home. The song itself surfaced on Everyday (1993), after his stint with the band had already ended.

origins.title: Bear's Gone Fishin'
origins.text: It started life as an instrumental in the summer of 1997; the lyrics didn't arrive until a couple of years later. The name belongs to a friend of the band — Colorado's Thomas “Bear” Guenther, who passed in 2019 — which is exactly why it reads as a near-lock whenever Panic sets up at Red Rocks. It found a studio home on 'Til the Medicine Takes.

sources.label: Widespread Panic at Red Rocks feature (Bear Guenther, instrumental-to-lyrics timeline)

origins.title: Surprise Valley
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (a signature Michael Houser piece)
origins.text: One of Michael Houser's signature pieces, and another of the band's Colorado love letters — the state Dave Schools has called a second home. It arrived on 'Til the Medicine Takes (1999) and never really left the rotation.

sources.label: “7 Widespread Panic Stories Told By the Band” (Houser signature song; Colorado connection)

origins.title: Big Wooly Mammoth
origins.text: A JoJo Hermann showcase — that's him on the vocal — and a live favorite fans hollered for long before it finally got a proper studio cut on Don't Tell the Band (2001).

sources.label: Don't Tell the Band (JoJo Hermann vocal; long-time crowd favorite)

origins.title: Blue Indian
origins.writtenBy: Widespread Panic (counted among Michael Houser's songs)
origins.text: If you've ever wondered where the album title 'Til the Medicine Takes comes from — it's right here, lifted straight out of this song's chorus. Counted among Michael Houser's writing, “Blue Indian” sits fourth on that 1999 record.

sources.label: 'Til the Medicine Takes (album title drawn from the chorus)

origins.title: Send Your Mind
origins.text: Not a Panic tune at all — it's a Van Morrison cover, drawn from the loose, funky sides Morrison cut for Bang Records in the late '60s. Panic worked up their own version for the 1991 self-titled record (“Mom's Kitchen”).

origins.title: Sleeping Man
origins.text: One of two Vic Chesnutt songs Panic carried into their sets — Chesnutt being the singular Athens songwriter the band kept close for years. His original turned up on 1993's Drunk; Panic's live reading later made it onto Live in the Classic City II.

origins.title: Aunt Avis
origins.text: The other Vic Chesnutt song — and the one that stuck. Panic debuted their reading of the Athens songwriter's tune on 9/9/95 and it settled in as a fixture; the studio version landed on Bombs & Butterflies (1997). The 1997 video is a genuine curio: it was directed by Billy Bob Thornton. Thornton was no stranger to the band — he'd also directed a 1991 Widespread Panic documentary — and the clip, shot in Arkansas, roped in Laura Dern and Vic Chesnutt himself.

sources.label: Debut date & Billy Bob Thornton-directed video
Billy Bob Thornton video details (Laura Dern; Arkansas; 1991 WSP documentary)
sources.publisher: Centerstage (2001) & South Bend Tribune (1997), via Spreadnet

origins.title: Bowlegged Woman
origins.text: A down-and-dirty blues cover — written by Calvin Carter and bluesman Bobby Rush, and sometimes billed with its fuller title, “Bowlegged Woman, Knock-Kneed Man.” Hot Tuna cut a version back in the '70s, which may well be the road it took to Panic; the band's own take shows up on Live at Myrtle Beach.

origins.title: Henry Parsons Died
origins.text: It landed on Panic's Everyday (1993), but the song belongs to Bloodkin — the Athens songwriting outfit of Daniel Hutchens and Eric Carter, longtime friends of the band whose catalog Panic raided more than once. A cover on paper; a family affair in practice. Dave Schools puts the subject matter plainly: it's a song about a very, very bad man.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Chainsaw City
origins.text: A Jerry Joseph song — like “Climb to Safety” and “North,” another piece of Joseph's writing Panic made its own. The original appeared on 1990's Pretty Wiped Out by Joseph's band, Little Women.

origins.title: Quarter Tank Of Gasoline
origins.text: Easy to mistake for a Jerry Joseph tune, but it's actually Bloodkin — Daniel Hutchens' writing, off the band's 1994 record Good Luck Charm. One more the band borrowed from their Athens running mates.

origins.title: Party At Your Mama's House
origins.text: An instrumental that carries a second, funkier name on the band's own alias sheet — “That Thang.” It got room to stretch on 'Til the Medicine Takes (1999) and later on Über Cobra. First played 5/7/97.

sources.label: Everyday Companion — Song Histories (instrumental; a.k.a. “That Thang”)

origins.title: Little Lilly
origins.text: The song that kicks off Don't Tell the Band (2001) — and one that hides two other names. It's often listed in full as “Little Lilly (A.K.A. First Snow Or Blue Girl).” Listen for JB tipping his hat to the Beatles' “She Came In Through the Bathroom Window,” quietly swapping the bathroom for a bedroom. First played 11/17/98.

sources.label: Don't Tell the Band review (Beatles-lyric wink)
Full title / alternate names (“First Snow,” “Blue Girl”)
sources.publisher: lyric databases; corroborated by fan alias sheets

origins.title: Down
origins.text: A milestone as much as a song. Don't Tell the Band (2001) is where Todd Nance stepped out from behind the kit as a genuine lead singer — and “Down” is the one that put him there. First played 4/16/00.

sources.label: Don't Tell the Band review (Nance's lead-vocal emergence)

origins.title: Give
origins.text: Built on one of Michael Houser's most aggressive guitar figures, “Give” got pegged at release as maybe the most commercially potent original Panic had — a heavy, direct thing in a catalog that usually prefers to wander. From Don't Tell the Band (2001); first played 4/7/00.

origins.title: Casa Del Grillo
origins.text: “House of the Cricket,” give or take, out of the Spanish. It's the band leaning all the way into the Latin thread Domingo “Sunny” Ortiz has run through Panic's music from the very start — Spanish lyrics and all — with John Keane layering some extra guitar effects onto the studio take. From Don't Tell the Band (2001); first played 7/16/00.

sources.label: Don't Tell the Band review (Spanish lyrics; Ortiz's Latin influence)

origins.title: Sleepy Monkey
origins.text: Deep-roots trivia. “Sleepy Monkey” is bound up with the band's very first record: in 1986 they self-released a 45 on their own Space Baby label — all of 500 copies — carrying a shortened version of this tune, “Monkey Image,” on the B-side. JB has told the story of driving in from their King Avenue house to Atlanta to pick the box of singles up from the presser. The song itself reaches back to Panic's earliest days; it first turned up on a stage on 2/24/85, the same night the band's whole story starts.

sources.label: Widespread Panic debut-single reissue (Space Baby label, 500 copies, JB's account)

origins.title: Wondering
origins.text: A Michael Houser song the band sat on for a while — nobody quite knew what to do with it until the Everyday sessions, when they finally wrestled it into shape in the studio. Dave Schools has said the take that made the record came somewhere around the forty-eighth pass; they just kept hammering at the arrangement until it clicked. First played 2/7/92.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Pilgrims
origins.text: A quiet, hypnotic one. Dave Schools holds a single low pedal tone underneath while the chords shift overhead — which is exactly the spell it casts. He's described it as a song about riding town to town: one radio station fading out as the next fades in, until the radio itself becomes the song. Road music in the truest sense. First played 9/14/92.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Makes Sense To Me
origins.text: Another one out of the Bloodkin songbook. Danny Hutchens wrote it for his own band, then decided it sounded more like Widespread than Bloodkin — so he handed it over, and it's been in Panic's hands ever since. First played 4/1/91.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Can't Get High
origins.text: One more Danny Hutchens / Bloodkin tune — and, briefly, a real calling card. It was the band's radio single, the song they took to Late Night with Conan O'Brien in 1995, horn section and all. Schools boils the lyric down to the ache of not being able to feel any elation because whatever you'd need for it — a person, a pet, some sleep — just isn't there. First played 7/9/93.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Junior
origins.text: Not one song but a medley — Panic's stitch-up of two or three tunes by the north Mississippi hill-country blues great Junior Kimbrough. Schools has called it a pure rocket-blues song. A deep-South nod dropped square into the middle of a set. First played 4/9/93.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Love Tractor
origins.text: Named, affectionately, after another band. Panic had a lick they liked to jam on that kept coming out sounding like the great '80s Athens group Love Tractor — so much so that they just called it “That Love Tractor Thing” until JB built it into an actual song about, fittingly, a guy who loves riding his tractor. One of the most-played things in the entire catalog. First played 6/16/89.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Dream Song
origins.text: A lullaby, and a tender one: Michael Houser wrote it for his son, Waker, when Waker was a baby. Dave Schools plays fretless on it — a soft touch for a soft song. First played 3/13/92.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Little Kin
origins.text: One of JB's, and — by his bandmate's own account — a deeply personal one, the kind Dave Schools says he won't try to explain too far. Schools has admitted it lands hard for him as an adopted child: the wrenching cry near the end, the image of a child who's inherited a parent's eyes and a parent's hands. A slab of raw funk carrying a whole lot of feeling. First played 4/24/93.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Vacation
origins.text: One of the great lost-in-plain-sight Panic songs. By 1997 they'd been playing “Vacation” live, on and off, for the better part of eight years — and had still never recorded it. It nearly made Bombs & Butterflies: the band cut one take, John Keane came back over the monitor to report it had run nine and a half minutes, and that was that. Too big to fit on a record. First played all the way back on 9/15/86.

sources.label: Dave Schools, The Music Monitor #94 (Pat Lall), May 1997, via Spreadnet
